title: Modified duration from Macaulay duration
prompt: A bond paying semiannual coupons has a Macaulay duration of 7.0 and a yield to maturity of 6%. Compute its modified duration and interpret the number.
worked_steps: Identify the inputs: Macaulay duration = 7.0, annual yield = 6% with semiannual compounding.
Convert to the periodic yield: 6% / 2 = 3% per period.
Interpret: the bond's price falls roughly 6.80% for a 100 bp rise in yield (and rises about as much for a 100 bp fall).
rationale: Macaulay duration is a weighted-average time; dividing by (1 + periodic yield) rescales it into a price sensitivity, so use the per-period yield, never the annual one.

source.passage: Modified duration equals Macaulay duration divided by one plus the yield per period.

title: Duration relations
prompt: Fill in the duration relations for an option-free bond.
cloze_text: Macaulay duration is the {{c1::weighted-average time to receipt}} of the bond's cash flows. Modified duration = Macaulay duration / {{c2::(1 + periodic yield)}}. A 100 bp yield rise cuts the price by roughly {{c3::modified duration}} percent.
rationale: The chain runs time-measure to slope: Macaulay averages when cash arrives, and one period of discounting turns it into the price sensitivity that scales linearly with the yield shock.

source.passage: Modified duration equals Macaulay duration divided by one plus the yield per period.

title: Approximate price change
prompt: Approximate a bond's price response to a small yield change.
cloze_text: %change in price is approximately {{c1::minus modified duration}} times {{c2::the change in yield}}; the minus sign encodes the inverse price-yield relation.
rationale: Duration is a first-order (linear) approximation: it captures the slope of the price-yield curve, understates gains and overstates losses because convexity is ignored.

source.passage: The percentage price change is approximately minus the modified duration multiplied by the change in yield.

title: Callable bond duration measure
stem: An analyst must estimate the interest-rate risk of a callable corporate bond. The most appropriate duration measure is:
rationale: A call option makes the bond's cash flows change when rates move, so the measure must reprice the bond off shifted benchmark curves: that is effective duration.

distractor_rationales.A: Modified duration assumes the cash flows are fixed; a callable's cash flows shorten when rates fall, so it misprices exactly the scenarios that matter.
distractor_rationales.C: Macaulay duration is a weighted-average time to receipt, not a price sensitivity, and it too assumes option-free cash flows.

source.passage: Bonds with embedded options require effective duration, computed from parallel shifts of the benchmark yield curve.

title: Duration price estimate
stem: A bond has a modified duration of 6.5. If its yield rises by 50 bp, the approximate percentage price change is closest to:
rationale: %change = -ModDur x change in yield = -6.5 x 0.005 = -3.25%: scale the full-point sensitivity down to the 50 bp shock and keep the minus sign.

distractor_rationales.B: Sign flip: price and yield move inversely, so a yield rise must produce a price fall.
distractor_rationales.C: Shock-scaling error: -6.50% is the response to a full 100 bp move, not the 50 bp move given.

source.passage: The percentage price change is approximately minus the modified duration multiplied by the change in yield.

title: Macaulay vs modified duration
left_body: Weighted-average time to receipt of the bond's cash flows, in years. Weights = present value of each cash flow / bond price.
right_body: Approximate percentage price change for a 1% yield change. ModDur = MacDur / (1 + periodic yield).
discriminator: One is a time, the other is a slope: which one prices a 75 bp yield shock, and how do you derive it from the other?
rationale: Use modified duration: %change = -ModDur x 0.0075. It comes from Macaulay duration by dividing by (1 + periodic yield); Macaulay itself only answers when, on average, the cash arrives.

source.passage: Modified duration equals Macaulay duration divided by one plus the yield per period.

title: Present value of an ordinary annuity
prompt: An investment pays 1,000 at the end of each year for 3 years. The required rate of return is 8%. Compute the present value.
worked_steps: Write the annuity PV factor: (1 - (1 + r)^-N) / r with r = 0.08, N = 3.
rationale: An ordinary annuity discounts each end-of-period payment back to today; the closed-form factor just sums the three individual discountings, so the PV must be below 3,000.

source.passage: The present value of an ordinary annuity equals the payment times one minus the inverse growth factor, divided by the periodic rate.
